titlestmt.titleproper: Rochella Thorpe oral history project files7607
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Sarah Keen

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2021-02-24 18:24:53 -0500.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Rochella Thorpe oral history project files
did.abstract: Tape recordings and transcripts of interviews with lesbians, mostly African-American and white, who lived in Detroit in the post-World War II period, 1947-1975.

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: As a doctoral student in the History Department of Binghamton University, Rochella (Roey) Thorpe researched lesbian life in Detroit, 1947-1975. She looked at the quite separate environments of African-American and white lesbians created in the Post War era. Within racial groups, lesbians mostly associated with women of similar economic backgrounds. Rather than finding one homogenous lesbian community, Thorpe found many different lesbian communities, and looked at the ways these communities and their survival strategies changed over time.

Her study is based in Detroit, a city that saw great urban growth and racial tension prior to and during World War II. Women's work in Detroit's wartime factories represented significant changes for both white and African-American women. After World War II, the fear of spreading communism in the automobile unions made Detroit the site of the 1951 House UnAmerican Activities Committee investigations. Detroit was a Midwest center for the civil rights, anti-war, and feminist movements of the 1960s and the emergence of the gay liberation movement in the 1970s. In 1970, Detroit women formed a chapter of the Daughters of Bilitis, and the radical newspaper THE DETROIT GAY LIBERATOR began publishing.

Thorpe's study includes substantial interviews with lesbians who lived in Detroit over this time period. Thorpe was the first openly lesbian candidate to be elected to the City of Ithaca (N.Y.) Common Council. The 5th Ward elected her as Alderwoman for a term from 1994 through 1997.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Tape recordings of interviews with fifty lesbians, mostly African-American and white, who lived in Detroit in the post-World War II period, 1947-1975. Transcripts are available for twenty-three interviews.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

defitem.label: EAD encoding:
defitem.item: Andrea HektorPeter Martinez, June 2003Sarah Keen, November 2008

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Box 2 contains copies of transcripts in box 1. Patrons should use and make reproductions from items in box 2.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Due to the fragility and potential degradation of moving image and sound recordings, viewing and listening is limited to items that have been digitized. If an item is in another media format, you may request to have the item digitized for access. Information on ordering access copies may be found on the Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections webpage. All audio visual material in this collection has been digitized.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Rochella Thorpe oral history project files, #7607. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

controlaccess.subject: African American lesbians -- Michigan -- Detroit -- Social life and customs.
Lesbians -- Michigan -- Detroit -- Social life and customs.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Any patron copies should be made from the items in box 2.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Any patron copies should be made from the items in this box.

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: All of the tapes in this collection have been digitized, for access please contact RMC staff.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Note on case:"this tape is a conversation between [Roey], JB, Caerdwyn, Mari, and a woman who prefers to remain anonymous (trades woman)."
